Bonus Q&A Step 5:
The Grand Offer,Part 1

Jon: 	Lisa, absolutely, you can use stock photos. You should use stock
photos of happy kids, absolutely. That is a great question.
“What if the price is very high,” Richard asks. “What would be a good
tease phrase?” Well, it doesn’t matter. You can use exactly what we
just said. We’re going to go through the price countdown in a moment,
where you build the value of the price up even higher than it is. It’s
called a faux price. We’re going to dive into that right in step 2. That’s
going to really be interesting to you.
So, Richard, don’t worry about that. If the price is very high, chances
are they’re probably aware of that. Most people coming to a video
sales letter are probably aware of it.
So, if you get to the price, you can just slightly reword it. But you can
still say, “You know what? It should cost more than it does. I’ll get to
the price in just a moment.” That’s really all you want to do.
All you’re doing, at this point, is say, “You may be curious about the
price. Don’t worry, I’m going to get to that in just a moment. First…”
That’s all you really need to say. I elaborated on the slides to show
you wordsmithing.
Pam asked a great question, and it’s something I cover in recording
your VSL. “Have you tested the font you use, versus others?”
Absolutely. By far and away, San Serif font, Helvetica, Arial, Tahoma
– I like Calibri, it’s just kind of a nice font – perform better than Times
New Roman or something cursive, or that kind of thing.

2
But, that being said, if your product lends itself like, to be honest with
you, I think Leslie’s product would totally do this, if it lends itself to
being a little bit whimsical or a little bit lighthearted, even a diet product
could be a little bit lighthearted.
I’ve seen handwritten fonts, not hard-to-read cursive, but the
handwritten font do really, really well. It gives more a live feel.
Everyone do me a favor and don’t say “Comic Sans.” No, not Comic
Sans! That’s a little too lighthearted. I’ll tell you what. I will find some
fonts for you that I would suggest.
Leslie, I didn’t mean to come down hard on you, but that’s the graphic
artist in me, for 17 years, coming out and going, “Oh, no, not that font!”
There’s only one font on Earth, to me, that is a blasphemous font, and I
think I’ve struck it out of my memory, it’s just such an awful font. And I
see people using it on invitations. It’s one of Mac’s original. It’s
supposed to be kind of fancy font, but it’s really awful. Josh, what’s the
name of that font?
But yes, test the fonts. That’s cool. If everybody forgives me, just say,
“I forgive you. I forgive you.” Make me feel better. Say, “I forgive you
for putting it in the wrong place.”
Brad asks, “Have you ever tested telling people earlier on there will be
a price for this?” A couple of years ago, Keith Wellman’s VSL course
suggested saying this in the first few minutes, “The price will surprise
you”?
No, no, no. Have I tested it? Yeah. Does it work? No. In my opinion,
and I’m not trying to bash Keith here, but in my opinion, Keith’s stuff
doesn’t deal with rapport. This is a complete rapport issue.
If you talk about price right off the bat, unless – now, there is a caveat
to this – they know that you’re going to something where they’re going
to buy.
So, in a webinar format or if it’s a VSL webinar-style thing, or if it’s
commons sense, like if they get to something where they’re saying,
“Today, we’re going to talk about software that you can get blah, blah,
blah,” then you would say something like that, for sure. Absolutely. I
hope that helped.
“Is the VSL marketed as a webinar that is attended live?” Thanks for
the forgiveness. Thank you. Great question, Laurie. Great question,
and it’s been answered for you in a tutorial that I created over the
weekend. As we promised, I created a tutorial for you guys as a

Josh: 	Adam is noticing, right now, and wanted to confirm that this process
that you’re going through for the 3X VSL is basically the same or
similar for a live event, for coaching programs, that you can use it live
events to sell coaching programs, and you can sell paid webinar
trainings, things like this, and not just digital products online.
Jon: 	Absolutely. Again, I’ve said this from the start. I had a viewer last
week, who said, “I want a refund, because this will never work for my
business.”
So, I emailed the guy and said, “Okay, no problem giving you a refund.
No problem at all. But I want to know what your business is, because I
want to show you how it will work.”
The guy never emailed me back. So, in other words, he just needed
the cash.
That’s going to happen. You’re going to get scumbags along the way.
If somebody’s trying to rip me off of something I’m doing, I call them a
scumbag. So, shoot me at sunrise.
And maybe that’s not the case. Maybe he just didn’t get the email.

-- 4 of 7 --

5
But the point is it will work for anyone. It will work for anyone. I’ve yet
to have somebody present a business to me where this did not work, a
service, anything. It will work.
Does it take a little bit of clever brainstorming to get around? Sure.
Absolutely. That’s why we made the formula pretty flexible. That’s
why there’s so many steps in each process, because you can go
through these steps and go, “You know, that step won’t work for me,”
or “I think I can put that step here.” And that’s fine. That’s fine.
This is why it’s so vast. And this is why I’m also showing you, slide by
slide, by slide, which you will get tonight, which you don’t have up right
now, by the way, because I didn’t want it up right now. What you will
get tonight is the final PowerPoint slides with everything combined.
So, you will literally have a templated video sales letter, plus a PDF of
the templated video sales letter, plus a PDF of the templated video
sales letter, with all of the notes in between. So, you can literally go
slide by slide, by slide, by slide.
You can use the general wording. Don’t use it specifically, just
because it would sound a little bit off. But I want you guys to get good
at writing on your own. But you can see how I’m doing it and you can
see how I’m thinking, and then you can just take those ideas and say,
“This will work, and this won’t work.”
But as far as working for a business? Any business. Any business.
Josh: 	Okay, here’s a great question from Richard. Richard’s wondering if
you have a brand new product that you don’t have any testimonials for
yet, maybe no one’s really tried it out that much, is there any kind of
replacement you can use for testimonials? Is there something you can
put there instead? What is your advice on that?
Jon: 	Expert testimonials. I’m going to cover that later, too. I have expert
testimonials. So, what you want to do is go out and get colleagues in
your field, who are experts in your field, to say your product is great.
If you don’t have that, skip testimonials altogether. I’ve done video
sales letters without one testimonial. Without one. And it wasn’t
because I didn’t have one, it was because I was lazy. 	Honestly, it was
because I didn’t have this formula in front of me.
When I said, “Let’s formulate this thing, so I don’t have to keep
reinventing the wheel,” I would go back through some of my old video
sales letters – this is from like 2 years ago – and go, “Oh my gosh, I
completely left off testimonials!” or “Wow, I didn’t do a price drop!”
Now, I use this all the time. Just FYI.

-- 5 of 7 --

6
Worst-case scenario, no testimonials. Social proof helps. But let me
give you a tip, real quick.
Let me give you a real good tip: recruit 20 people. Recruit 20 people.
By the way, speaking of recruiting people, the folks that my assistant
emailed about the video testimonials, whoever can get back to her,
please, today. That would be awesome. Thank you, guys, for doing
that.
So, here’s what you want to do. Recruit 20 people and give them the
product. And make the only stipulation is that they give you their
honest feedback on the product.
And out of those 20 people, hopefully you’ll get 15 that will give you
glowing reviews. Then, you’ve got all the testimonials that you’ll ever
need.
Josh: 	Another great question from Leslie. She’s actually the last question up
here. If you have any more, please post them.
It says, “If you have product versions, or different sections of your
products, and she has 5 different parties that you might want to plan,
should you get product shots for each one, or should you use a giant
box that has all of the snip-its kind of thing?
Jon: 	What I would do for that, and there’s a lot of products that have
multiple components, I call it the big box. You want a big box graphic.
So, have a graphic designer create a big box graphic.
Obviously, if it’s a party thing, it’s probably not in a big box. Or, maybe
it is, and you can take a picture of it. I don’t know.
But then, you can say, “I’ll show you what each of these components
are. You get this, and this does this.” And you always want to give a
value for what that is.
“If I sold that by itself, it would be $400,” or whatever. You’re tallying
up the value of the product. And by the time you give the price, they’ll
see what a good deal it is.
I use the word “value,” by the way. I use the word “value,” unless
there’s a physical price. For example, there’s several upsells that I
have, where I say, “If you don’t believe me, go to this website. You
can see that it costs $17. I’m giving it to you for free.”
I just called a guy up and go, “I know you’re selling these, but can I
give it away as a bonus?”

-- 6 of 7 --

7
I do that for my own stuff. I sell Empower Audio, for $97, and I give
that 12 audios away for free.
So, I said, “This is what I sell for $97. If you don’t believe me, go here.”
But if you don’t have a set price, and people do this all the time, make
up a value.
Don’t make up an outlandish value. But all of the stuff that you’re
about to see, when I get to my bonuses, are real. They’re the real
values. And what I say is, “If I were to sell you this, this is what you
would pay for it.”
This is important for FTC reasons, by the way. I’m not saying they’re
going to come crashing on you. They haven’t come crashing on
anyone that I know of, that is all talk, right now. But just in case, I think
it’s a good policy to follow, just from ethics.
The FTC is saying, “You can no longer say that the bonuses are
included in your office. In other words, if you have an offer that’s $97,
and you say, “Okay, so you’re going to get $5,000 in bonuses.” So, in
reality, this offer is $5,097, but you’re only going to pay $97.” That’s
not true, because you’re giving them the $5,000.
So, the wording to get around that is to say, “If you were to buy these
separately, it would cost $5,000,” or “The value of this is $5,000.
You’re getting the value of a $5,097 product for $97.”
It’s, again, wordsmithing. But in this case, it’s legal wordsmithing.

-- 7 of 7 --
